---
title: "Building the alldata species sensitivity dataset"
subtitle: "Pipeline methods, decisions, and deviations from the ANZG derivation procedure"
author: "ssddata authors"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Building the alldata species sensitivity dataset}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

## Overview

`ssd_data_sets(set = "alldata")` returns a single aggregated dataset of aquatic
toxicity values, one per species per chemical per medium, drawn from multiple
ecotoxicological databases and curated datasets. This vignette describes how that
dataset is constructed: which sources contribute, what transformations are applied,
and why specific methodological choices were made.

The aggregation procedure follows the method of Warne et al. (2025), the
authoritative technical reference for deriving Australian and New Zealand water
quality guideline values (ANZG guidelines). Key sections of that document are
cited throughout. Where this pipeline deviates from the Warne et al. (2025)
procedure — whether because of data limitations, because it produces a dataset
for methodology testing rather than official guideline derivation, or because of
deliberate conservative choices — the deviations are documented explicitly in
the [Deviations appendix](#sec-deviations).

---

## Data sources {#sec-sources}

The `alldata` dataset integrates seven sources, divided into curated and uncurated
categories.

**Curated sources** have been prepared and quality-assured by the organisations
that hold them. They are used as-is, subject only to schema harmonisation and
deduplication:

- **ANZG** — Australian and New Zealand Guidelines dataset; highest priority in
  the deduplication hierarchy.
- **CCME** — Canadian Council of Ministers of the Environment dataset.
- **AIMS** — Australian Institute of Marine Science dataset.
- **CSIRO** — Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation dataset.

**Uncurated sources** are large, heterogeneous databases. They require more
extensive pre-processing before they can be aggregated:

- **anztox** — a PostgreSQL database of ecotoxicological data maintained
  internally; accessed via a live database connection (Windows Positron only).
- **wqbench** — an R package providing access to the ECOTOX database
  (Chapman et al.); intercepted before the package's own aggregation step runs,
  so that raw records are available for deduplication against other sources.
- **envirotox** — the EnviroTox database, provided as an Excel workbook.

The `anon_` datasets distributed with the package are excluded from `alldata`
because they lack chemical identifiers.

---

## Data flow {#sec-dataflow}

The pipeline processes uncurated and curated sources on separate tracks that
merge at Stage 6. The table below summarises the row counts at each major
transformation for the uncurated track. Numbers marked `<!-- STAGEXX: ... -->`
will be confirmed from audit reports as each stage completes.

| Stage | Operation | Rows in | Rows out |
|-------|-----------|---------|----------|
| 4b | Extract raw records (uncurated) | — | 449,888 |
| 4c | Deduplication + priority selection | 449,888 | 381,410 |
| 4d | Taxonomy resolution + synonym unification | 381,410 | 381,382 |
| 4e | Aggregation (one value per species × chemical × medium) | 381,382 | <!-- STAGE4E: replace with confirmed output row count --> |
| 6 | Integration with curated sources + ANZG exclusion rule | — | <!-- STAGE6: replace --> |
| 7 | Sufficiency filtering (≥5 species / ≥4 groups) | — | <!-- STAGE7: replace --> |

---

## Stage 1 — Schema audit {#sec-stage1}

Before any data were extracted, a schema audit documented the column names,
data types, and value vocabularies in each of the three uncurated sources. The
audit identified that the fields required by the Warne et al. (2025) Section
3.4.4 aggregation procedure — specifically `statistic_type`, `duration_hours`,
`effect_category`, and `life_stage` — were available in varying forms across the
three sources and would require harmonisation.

The audit output is `scripts/stage1-schema-audit.md`.

---

## Stage 2 — CAS number and chemical name alignment {#sec-stage2}

Different sources use different CAS numbers for the same chemical (e.g.
speciation variants of metals, salt forms of organic compounds). A parent-CAS
lookup table maps source-specific CAS numbers to a canonical parent CAS number
and chemical name for each chemical group.

The lookup was built in five sub-stages (2a–2e), combining:

- A 40-row human-curated seed lookup for known speciation cases.
- Heuristic rules that identify CAS numbers sharing a parent by name pattern.
- LLM-assisted enrichment of ambiguous cases.
- Manual review of uncertain assignments.

The master lookup is `data-raw/cas_parent_lookup_all.csv` (587 rows). Eighteen
rows remain flagged UNCERTAIN pending domain expert review; these affect Stage 6
deduplication and are documented in the deferred decisions log
(`scripts/stage3-deferred-decisions.md`).

Speciation decisions and rules are documented in
`data-raw/anztox/speciation_convention.md` and
`scripts/speciation_policy_extensions.md`.

The build process for this lookup is documented in full in
`vignettes/cas_parent_lookup_build.qmd`.

---

## Stage 3 — Media assignment {#sec-stage3}

Each record is assigned to a medium: Freshwater, Marine, or Unknown. The ANZG
guideline values are derived separately for freshwater and marine environments,
so medium assignment is a prerequisite for all subsequent grouping and filtering.

- **anztox**: medium is available as a controlled field; values are normalised
  to the three-value vocabulary.
- **wqbench**: medium was added via PR #43 (`add_media_wqbench`).
- **envirotox**: medium is not reliably available; all envirotox records are
  assigned "Unknown".
- **ccme**: medium assignment is pending resolution of Issue #34; all ccme
  records are interim "Unknown".

The ANZG medium field uses five variants (hard/moderate/soft freshwater, marine,
estuarine freshwater). These are not collapsed — the distinctions are preserved
for downstream use.

---

## Stage 4
### a — Pipeline audit (uncurated sources) {#sec-stage4a}

A targeted audit of each uncurated source's extraction pipeline established the
following:

- **anztox**: concentration units for the `concentrationused` field are µg/L for
  the 2000 dataset subset (86% of records, confirmed via supplementary DB query)
  and assumed µg/L for the 2016 dataset subset (14%), with uncertainty flagged
  via a `source_dataset` column.
- **wqbench**: must be intercepted before `wqb_aggregate()` runs so that raw
  records are available for deduplication; the package's own aggregation is not
  used.
- **envirotox**: the DATASET.R solubility and type filter is reproduced at
  interception to ensure the same record set.
- 632 "Chronic QSAR" rows in anztox were deliberately excluded (modelled values,
  not experimental data).

The full audit is `scripts/stage4a-pipeline-audit.md`.

---

### b — Extract filtered unaggregated records {#sec-stage4b}

Raw unaggregated records were extracted from all three uncurated sources into a
common 17-column schema (`uncurated_raw_combined.csv`). The schema includes all
fields required by the Section 3.4.4 aggregation: `effect_category`,
`statistic_type`, `duration_hours`, and `life_stage`, as well as the `acr_eligible`
flag (TRUE where `statistic_type` ∈ {EC50, LC50, IC50}).

Source record counts after extraction: anztox 15,667 / wqbench 361,782 /
envirotox 72,439. Total: 449,888 rows.

The extraction script is `scripts/stage4b-extract.R` but requires access to the original anztox SQL database.

---

### c — Cross-source deduplication {#sec-stage4c}

#### Why deduplication before priority selection?

A key architectural decision (Decision I2) is that cross-source deduplication
is applied *before* ANZG priority selection (chronic > subchronic > acute within
a source). The alternative — applying priority selection first, then deduplicating
— risks discarding chronic data from a lower-priority source in favour of acute
data from a higher-priority source. The chosen order ensures that a chronic
record from, say, envirotox is not lost because an acute record for the same
observation exists in a higher-priority source.

#### Deduplication procedure

Cross-source duplicates were identified using a six-field key:

`native_cas × scientificname_norm × medium × statistic_type_norm × effect_category × duration_hours × conc_ug_L`

where `conc_ug_L` was matched with a 0.1% tolerance to accommodate floating-point
representation differences. `NA` values in key fields were treated strictly — a
row with NA duration does not match a row with a non-NA duration.

For true cross-source duplicates, one record is retained per the priority order:
`wqbench > anztox > envirotox` (for the uncurated sources). All records are
flagged rather than dropped, so the full record of what was found is preserved.

Within-source duplicates were detected and flagged separately (anztox 9.2%,
wqbench 25.1%, envirotox 0.56%). The wqbench within-source duplicate rate is
intrinsic to the package's prepared-dataset structure and is not treated as a
defect.

After deduplication and priority selection, the final clean subset contains
381,410 rows. The audit report is `data-raw/alldata/stage4c-dedup-report.md`.

#### Effect category harmonisation

The three sources use incompatible effect category vocabularies. A controlled
vocabulary of 13 values was established:

> MORT, GRO, REP, IMM, DVP, HAT, PSE, POP, LUM, ABD, BEH, BCH, MOR

wqbench uses English-word categories (e.g. "Mortality", "Growth") that were
mapped to this vocabulary via `scripts/stage4c-effect-category-fixup.R`. The
mapping is `data-raw/alldata/anztox_2016_effect_category_map.csv` and
`data-raw/alldata/envirotox_effect_category_map.csv`.

Approximately 23,567 records in the final clean subset have unmappable
`effect_category` values (primarily wqbench records with values such as
"Intoxication", "Multiple", and "Accumulation", and 175 anztox 2016 records
with values such as "PGR"). These records cannot enter the aggregation step
because `effect_category` is part of the grouping key; they are dropped in
Stage 4e with an explicit audit count.

---

### d — Species name resolution and taxonomy enrichment {#sec-stage4d}

#### Why resolve species names before aggregation?

The Section 3.4.4 geometric mean is computed per species. If a species appears
under different names in different sources (e.g. an accepted name in one source
and a synonym in another), those records will be treated as different species
unless synonyms are unified first. Stage 4d resolves this before aggregation.

This ordering reversal (species resolution before aggregation, not after) was
a deliberate design decision made during Stage 4d planning.

#### Resolution procedure

Species names were resolved against the World Register of Marine Species (WoRMS)
using a context-aware querying approach: source-native taxonomy (extracted in
Stage 4d Part 1.5 from each database's own taxonomy tables) was used to
disambiguate species that WoRMS returns multiple candidate matches for. Where
WoRMS could not resolve a species, the Global Biodiversity Information Facility
(GBIF) was used as a fallback, subject to a genus-or-finer rank floor.

Of 4,348 unique species names, 99.22% were resolved to a usable taxonomic level
(4,200 species). The remaining 148 were handled via a source-native fallback
(Stage 4d Part 2 fixup) or manual correction; 12 species remained unresolvable
(placeholder labels and unresolvable misspellings, all from anztox) and were
hard-excluded (28 rows removed).

#### Synonym unification

WoRMS resolution identified 254 synonym groups. The `accepted_name` column
in the enriched dataset carries the WoRMS-accepted name for all species in
a synonym group, replacing the raw source name. This affects 46,826 rows.
The `accepted_name` column is the authoritative species identifier for Stage 4e
aggregation; the original raw name is preserved in `original_scientificname`
for audit purposes.

#### Majorgroup derivation

The ANZG eligibility check requires species counts by taxonomic group. The
`majorgroup` column is derived directly from the resolved taxonomic class
(`class`), without reference to ANZG Table 5. This is a deliberate deviation
from strict ANZG procedure — see [Deviations appendix](#sec-deviations).

The enriched dataset (`uncurated_raw_dedup_enriched.csv`) contains 115 distinct
majorgroup values, with Teleostei, Branchiopoda, Malacostraca, Insecta, and
Chlorophyceae as the most represented groups.

The audit report is `data-raw/alldata/stage4d-part3-enrichment-report.md`.

---

### e — Aggregation: one toxicity value per species {#sec-stage4e}

The aggregation procedure follows Warne et al. (2025) Section 3.4.4. The goal
is to reduce the many raw toxicity records for each species to a single
representative toxicity value per species per chemical per medium.

#### Pre-aggregation preparation

Before aggregation, three preparatory steps are applied:

1. **Unit conversion**: wqbench records are in mg/L; all other sources are in
   µg/L. wqbench `conc_value` is multiplied by 1,000 before aggregation.

2. **Coerce artefact values**: the literal string "Not stated" in the
   `majorgroup`/`class` column (4 rows carrying a source-native placeholder)
   is coerced to NA.

3. **Drop records that cannot be aggregated**:
   - Records with NA `effect_category` (~23,567 rows) are dropped because
     `effect_category` is part of the grouping key.
   - Records where `test_class == "acute"` and `acr_eligible != TRUE` are
     dropped. These are acute NOECs, LOECs, and similar values that cannot
     be converted to chronic equivalents per Section 3.4.2.2 of Warne et al.
     (2025): *"acute EC/IC/LCx (where x ≠ 50), NOEC, LOEC and MATC values
     must not be used to derive chronic GVs"*.

#### Acute-to-chronic ratio conversion

Retained acute records with `acr_eligible == TRUE` (LC50, EC50, IC50 values)
are converted to chronic-equivalent values by dividing by the default
acute-to-chronic ratio (ACR) of 10, as specified in Section 3.4.2.2. The
`acr_applied` flag is set to TRUE for converted records.

The default ACR of 10 is used because chemical-specific or taxon-specific ACRs
are not available at pipeline scale. Warne et al. (2025) Section 3.4.2.2 states
that the default ACR of 10 *"should be used to convert acute LC/EC/IC/50 values
to chronic negligible-effect values"* in the absence of empirical ACRs.

#### Step 1: geometric mean within endpoint combinations

Following Section 3.4.4, the geometric mean is computed within each unique
combination of:

> `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium × effect_category × statistic_type × duration_hours × life_stage`

This grouping key implements the Section 3.4.4 requirement that geometric means
are not calculated across different statistical estimates of toxicity, life
stages, or test durations.

**Handling of NA in `life_stage` and `duration_hours`**: Both fields have
records where the value is not available (NA). NA is treated as a distinct
group level — it does not merge with any named value. A record with NA
life_stage and a record with life_stage "Neonate" are in separate groups,
even if all other key fields match. This is consistent with the conservative
approach applied throughout the pipeline: unknown values are not assumed to
be equivalent to known ones. Audit flags (`lifestage_mixed`, `duration_mixed`)
are set where both NA and non-NA values from a single source contribute to
the same endpoint minimum in Step 2.

**Geometric mean spread check**: If values within a group span more than one
order of magnitude (max/min > 10), the geometric mean is considered
unreliable (Warne et al. 2025, Section 3.4.4). In such cases, the minimum
value is used instead, and the `geomean_flagged` column is set to TRUE. This
implements the most conservative of the two options Warne et al. suggest for
professional judgement (selecting the lowest value).

#### Step 2: lowest value within each endpoint per species

Within each `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium × effect_category`
combination, the minimum is taken across all `statistic_type × duration_hours
× life_stage` combinations. This is the lowest geometric-mean value for that
endpoint.

#### Step 3: lowest value across endpoints per species

Within each `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium` combination, the
minimum is taken across all `effect_category` values. This is the single
toxicity value representing the sensitivity of the species for that chemical
in that medium.

#### Output

The Stage 4e output is one row per `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium`
combination. Key provenance columns are summarised:

- `n_records`: number of raw records that contributed to the aggregated value.
- `sources_contributing`: comma-separated list of source names.
- `any_acr_applied`: TRUE if any contributing record had ACR conversion applied.
- `geomean_flagged`: TRUE if any geometric mean was replaced by a minimum due
  to the >1 order of magnitude spread rule.

The output contains <!-- STAGE4E: replace with confirmed output row count --> rows
across <!-- STAGE4E: replace with confirmed n_chemicals --> chemicals and
<!-- STAGE4E: replace with confirmed n_species --> species.

The audit report is `data-raw/alldata/stage4e-aggregation-report.md`.

---

## Stage 5 — Units normalisation {#sec-stage5}

The wqbench mg/L → µg/L unit conversion was folded into Stage 4e (applied
before aggregation). A separate Stage 5 is not required.

---

## Stage 6 — Integration with curated sources {#sec-stage6}

*This stage is pending. This section will be completed after Stage 6 runs.*

The ANZG exclusion rule: where ANZG holds any freshwater data for a chemical,
all other sources' freshwater data for that chemical are excluded. This reflects
the primacy of ANZG's curated, quality-assured data.

The full deduplication preference order is:

> `anzg > ccme > aims > csiro > anztox > wqbench > envirotox`

<!-- STAGE6: add row counts, chemicals added by curated sources, ANZG exclusions applied -->

---

## Stage 7 — Sufficiency filtering {#sec-stage7}

*This stage is pending. This section will be completed after Stage 7 runs.*

A chemical × medium combination is eligible for inclusion in the `alldata` output
only if it has at least 5 species from at least 4 distinct taxonomic groups
(where groups are defined by `majorgroup` = `class`). Combinations that do not
meet this threshold are excluded.

<!-- STAGE7: add row counts, number of chemical × medium combinations passing and failing -->

---

## References {#sec-references}

Warne MStJ, Batley GE, van Dam RA, Chapman JC, Fox DR, Hickey CW, Stauber JL
and Fisher R (2025). *Method for deriving Australian and New Zealand water quality
guideline values for protecting aquatic ecosystems from toxicants — update of 2018
version*. Australian and New Zealand Guidelines for Fresh and Marine Water Quality.
CC BY 4.0. Australian and New Zealand Governments and Australian state and territory
governments, Canberra, ACT, Australia.

---

## Appendix A — Decisions table {#sec-decisions}

One row per named decision confirmed during pipeline planning. Decisions that
were reconsidered or reversed are noted; the most recent decision for each ID
is the operative one.

| Decision ID | Stage | Decision taken | Alternatives considered | Rationale | ANZG reference |
|-------------|-------|---------------|------------------------|-----------|----------------|
| I2 | 4c | Cross-source deduplication applied before ANZG priority selection | Apply priority selection first | Prevents acute data from a high-priority source displacing chronic data from a lower-priority source | — |
| G2 | 4c | Duplicate rows flagged and retained (not dropped) | Drop duplicates immediately | Preserves full audit trail; retained flag allows inspection | — |
| H2 | 4c | `native_cas` used as dedup key (not `casnumber_grouped`) | Use `casnumber_grouped` | At dedup time, parent grouping had not yet been applied | — |
| J1 | 4c | 0.1% concentration tolerance for cross-source duplicate matching | Exact match; higher tolerance | Accounts for floating-point representation differences without over-merging | — |
| J2b | 4c | NA in dedup key fields treated as non-matching (strict NA) | Treat NA as wildcard | Conservative; avoids false duplicate assignments | — |
| J3a | 4c | Medium treated strictly in dedup key | Ignore medium in dedup | Records from different media are not duplicates of each other | — |
| P3 | 4d | Single-source taxonomy pooling with wqbench > envirotox > anztox tiebreaker; fields not mixed across sources | Mix fields across sources | Avoids inconsistencies from combining taxonomy from different ontologies | — |
| R1 | 4d | "Genus sp." entries in uncurated sources resolved at genus level and retained | Drop "Genus sp."; resolve at species level | Genus-level taxonomy is recoverable and taxonomic information should be preserved | — |
| U3 | 4d | Source-native taxonomy used as fallback hierarchy for problem species; `accepted_name` never fabricated | Fabricate accepted_name from genus; drop problem species | Preserves data while maintaining honesty about resolution quality | — |
| D1 | 4e | NA `life_stage` treated as a distinct group level (not equivalent to any named value) | Drop NA life_stage rows; collapse NA with named values | Conservative; unknown life stage should not be assumed equivalent to a known one | §3.4.4 |
| D2 | 4e | Geometric mean replaced by minimum where within-group spread > 1 order of magnitude | Flag only; keep geometric mean; require manual review | Automated, reproducible, conservative; preserves audit trail via `geomean_flagged` | §3.4.4 |
| D3 | 4e | Eligibility check (≥5 species / ≥4 groups) deferred to Stage 7 | Apply in Stage 4e | Stage 4e output is a complete reference file; threshold is a single-point-of-control in Stage 7 | §3.5 |
| D4 | 4e | Per-row dedup audit columns dropped; provenance summarised as `n_records`, `sources_contributing`, `any_acr_applied`, `geomean_flagged` | Retain all input columns | Per-row dedup columns are not meaningful at the aggregated level | — |
| D5 | 4e | NA `duration_hours` treated as a distinct group level (199 rows retained, not dropped) | Drop NA duration rows | Consistent with NA life_stage treatment; records are legitimate and 199 rows is non-trivial | §3.4.4 |

---

## Appendix B — Deviations from Warne et al. (2025) {#sec-deviations}

This pipeline produces a dataset for SSD methodology testing, not for official
guideline derivation. Several deliberate deviations from strict ANZG procedure
are documented here. None of these deviations affect the scientific validity of
the dataset for its intended purpose.

| Deviation | Stage | Description | Justification | Consequence for use |
|-----------|-------|-------------|---------------|---------------------|
| `majorgroup` = taxonomic class | 4d | Taxonomic groups for eligibility checks are defined as the resolved WoRMS/GBIF class, not the groups in ANZG Table 5 | Simpler, reproducible, consistent with the `wqbench` package's own `filter(n_distinct(class) >= 4)` convention | Slight differences in which taxa are counted as distinct groups versus strict ANZG Table 5; does not affect SSD fitting |
| Default ACR applied at pipeline scale | 4e | A default ACR of 10 is applied to all eligible acute records; chemical-specific or taxon-specific ACRs are not computed | ACRs are not available at scale across thousands of chemicals; Warne et al. (2025) §3.4.2.2 specifies ACR = 10 as the default | Converted-acute values may be less accurate than values derived from empirical ACRs for specific chemicals |
| Eligibility threshold ≥5 species / ≥4 groups | 7 | The sufficiency threshold used for testing is ≥5 species from ≥4 taxonomic groups | More conservative than the ANZG minimum of ≥6 species; chosen deliberately to allow a wider range of chemicals in the test set | More chemicals pass the threshold than would under strict ANZG rules |
| WoRMS exact-name-priority pre-filter | 4d | Where WoRMS returns both a species record and a subspecies record for the same query name with identical taxonomy, the exact species match is promoted | WoRMS fuzzy=TRUE was conflating species and subspecies entries; the pre-filter prevents false ambiguity | Minor effect; affects a small number of species with subspecies records |
| GBIF genus-or-finer rank floor | 4d | GBIF matches are accepted only at genus rank or finer | Prevents higher-level matches (e.g. family or order) from being treated as species-level resolutions | A small number of species remain unresolved that a less strict floor would partially resolve |
| "Genus sp." entries retained at genus level (uncurated) | 4d | Entries of the form "Genus sp." are resolved to the genus level and retained, rather than dropped | Genus-level taxonomy is sufficient for majorgroup derivation and preserves data | These entries contribute to the species count at genus level; their toxicity values represent genus-level estimates |
